startScreenOptions.title: AN69.1-AN69.3 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN69.1-AN69.3.

interactions.label: Why does chronic hypertension predispose the aorta to dissection?

params.question: Why does chronic hypertension predispose the aorta to dissection?

answers.text: It causes progressive weakening of elastic fibres in the tunica media through repeated mechanical stress

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The aorta's tunica media is packed with elastic fibres that stretch with each heartbeat and recoil as pressure drops. Years of elevated pressure gradually weaken these fibres until the wall can no longer hold.

answers.text: It damages the endothelial lining of the tunica intima, exposing subendothelial collagen

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While endothelial damage can occur in hypertension, aortic dissection results from mechanical weakening of elastic fibres in the tunica media, not from endothelial disruption.

answers.text: It leads to fibrosis and thickening of the tunica adventitia, compressing the vasa vasorum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The adventitia contains vasa vasorum, but hypertension primarily damages the elastic fibres in the tunica media — the layer responsible for withstanding pulsatile pressure.

answers.text: It increases deposition of atherosclerotic plaque within the tunica media

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Atherosclerotic plaque accumulates in the tunica intima of muscular arteries. The aorta's dissection risk comes from mechanical weakening of elastic fibres in the media, not plaque deposition.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key is the tunica media — its elastic fibres stretch and recoil with each heartbeat, and chronic hypertension gradually weakens them through repeated mechanical stress until the wall can no longer hold.
Well done! The tunica media's elastic fibres bear the mechanical load of each heartbeat, and chronic hypertension progressively weakens them until the wall tears.

interactions.label: A histology slide shows an artery with approximately 50 concentric elastic sheet

params.question: A histology slide shows an artery with approximately 50 concentric elastic sheets in its tunica media, appearing wavy on H&E stain and giving a yellow colour on gross examination. Which type of artery is this?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Muscular arteries are identified by a prominent internal elastic lamina and 10 to 40 smooth muscle layers in the media, with sparse elastic fibres — not concentric elastic sheets.
Correct. Elastic arteries contain 40 to 70 concentric elastic lamellae in the tunica media. These sheets appear wavy on H&E staining and give the vessel a yellow colour grossly.
Incorrect. Arterioles are much smaller vessels (10–100 µm) with only 1 to 3 layers of smooth muscle and no concentric elastic sheets.

Incorrect. 'Distributing artery' is another name for muscular arteries, which have far fewer elastic lamellae and are defined instead by a prominent internal elastic lamina sandwiching a thick smooth-muscle wall.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key identifying feature is multiple concentric elastic sheets (40–70) in the tunica media. This distinguishes elastic arteries (aorta, common carotid) from muscular arteries, which have a prominent internal elastic lamina but far fewer elastic sheets.
Correct! Multiple concentric elastic lamellae in the media are the defining feature of elastic arteries like the aorta, enabling the Windkessel effect that smooths pulsatile blood flow.

interactions.label: A pathologist examines a capillary with wide gaps between endothelial cells, all

params.question: A pathologist examines a capillary with wide gaps between endothelial cells, allowing passage of whole blood cells and large proteins. In which organ is this capillary most likely found?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Skeletal muscle contains continuous capillaries with tight junctions between endothelial cells — the most restrictive capillary type, allowing only small molecules to pass.
Incorrect. The kidney glomerulus has fenestrated capillaries with small pores for rapid filtration, but these do not permit passage of whole blood cells.
Correct. The liver contains sinusoidal capillaries with wide lumens and gaps between endothelial cells, allowing whole blood cells and large proteins to pass through freely.
Incorrect. The lung has continuous capillaries that maintain a thin, tight barrier optimised for gas exchange, not for allowing cell passage.

overallFeedback.feedback: These are sinusoidal capillaries, found in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow. Their wide gaps between endothelial cells allow whole blood cells and large proteins to pass through — unlike the tighter continuous or fenestrated types.
Well done! Sinusoidal capillaries — found in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow — have the widest gaps, allowing passage of blood cells and large proteins.

metadata.title: A pathologist examines a capillary with

interactions.label: In diabetes, the basement membrane of blood vessels becomes thinner, leading to

params.question: In diabetes, the basement membrane of blood vessels becomes thinner, leading to increased permeability and microangiopathy.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. The basement membrane thickens in diabetes, not thins. Despite this thickening, the altered structure impairs normal filtration and exchange, causing the microvascular complications known as diabetic microangiopathy.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. In diabetes, the basement membrane actually thickens, not thins. This thickening disrupts normal filtration and molecular exchange, driving the microvascular complications known as diabetic microangiopathy.
